model_notes: The text states that slips occur in all languages, highlighting a universal property of human language.

The text discusses how errors provide insight into how people represent language mentally.
The text lists phonemes and syllables as units involved in errors, which are core concepts of phonology.
The text describes linguistic creativity (producing novel sentences) which is a key aspect of compositionality.
The text describes a multileveled architecture with distinct stages (grammatical vs phonological), supporting a modular view of language production.
The text contrasts naturalistic speech errors with experimental methods, touching on issues of reliability and ecological validity.
The text mentions speakers making errors even when reciting silently in their heads, a phenomenon of inner speech.
The text explicitly references the analysis of spontaneous speech errors as a root of language production research.
The text discusses the methodology of monitoring speakers' eye movements during production tasks.
The text explicitly mentions common ground in the context of audience design.
The text explicitly defines and discusses syntactic priming.
The text discusses the retrieval of phonological information, a key stage in production.
The text discusses speakers who converse in at least two languages within the Multilingualism section.
The text mentions linguists arguing for universal tendencies across languages.
The text discusses the great variability among languages.

The text contrasts language acquisition with learning a written language.

The text contrasts universal conversational features with linguistic structures that show variation.
The text discusses whether features of conversation are cross-culturally universal.
The text mentions normative principles of social conduct in conversation.
The text explicitly discusses research into common ground in conversation.

The text states that visual signals contribute critical semantic and pragmatic meaning.
